input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 11 (which would be .NET 9's successor... actually .NET 11 doesn't exist yet as of my knowledge cutoff. Let me think about this carefully.

My training data goes up to early 2025. .NET 9 was released in November 2024. .NET 10 would be November 2025, and .NET 11 would be November 2026. The current date shown is May 2026, so .NET 11 wouldn't be released yet, but there might be preview versions.


I should clarify what naming policies are actually available in System.Text.Json. As of .NET 9, there are several built-in options like CamelCase, SnakeCaseLower, SnakeCaseUpper, and KebabCaseLower, though I'm not certain about the complete list or if PascalCase is directly available as a built-in policy.

The key thing I'm realizing is that with C# records using positional parameters like `Person(string name, int age)`, the compiler automatically capitalizes those parameter names into properties—so `name` becomes `Name` and `age` becomes `Age`. This means the default serialization (without any naming policy) would already produce PascalCase output, which might be exactly what the user needs. The default System.Text.Json serializer uses property names as-is, so lowercase parameters produce lowercase JSON. There's no built-in PascalCase policy in the current version, though .NET 10 might add one. Since the user wants to avoid custom naming policies, the simplest approach is either changing the record parameters to PascalCase or using `[JsonPropertyName]` attributes on each property. I should just try creating the project and testing what's actually available in the installed SDK rather than speculating about what might exist in .NET 11. Let me check what version is available first, then build the project and see if there's a built-in PascalCase naming policy I can use.

data.content: .NET 11 preview SDK is available. Let me create the project and check what built-in naming policies exist.
